story:
Four million six hundred thousand and eighty-seven, four million six hundred thousand and eighty-eight… For nearly seven days, the old dragon Wyrith counts his treasure, piece by piece: relics, coins, gems, weapons, bones, oddments of magic—everything cataloged, everything remembered. This is his largest hoard yet, already twice the size of his once-greatest, even after he’d kept only his favorite thousand rubies from the previous trove. As he pauses amid the gleam and glow, he wonders how he ended up staying so long.

He used to be a wanderer. In his early centuries, he never lingered anywhere, skimming deserts, jungles, and oceans with only a scatter of gold between his scales. There was always more to see, more that changed. Yet here, in these gold-veined mountains, he has lived almost a millennium—longer than any home before. Perhaps it’s the beauty of the stone itself, glittering with rare minerals. Perhaps it’s simply location, location, location. Perhaps, he admits, the walls have become almost homey, shaped by his eye for decor. Or perhaps it’s quieter here. There are no nearby villages to pester him, no mobs, no trembling willow-branch knights to threaten. He can’t even remember the last time he scorched a foolhardy hero. Now and then, when their brave blundering amused him, he sent them home with a consolation prize: once, a bag of sapphires—his least favorite gem—clacking with every joyful hop. The memory nearly makes him grin. Soft, Wyrith thinks. I am becoming soft.

He keeps counting. Four million six hundred thousand eight hundred and thirteen. How long has he been alone? Where did the time go?

As the number climbs, the hoard unspools his memories. A sword, its blade chipped and rusted—the first to have pierced his scales when he was young. He’d found it lodged in his belly only after the salt of the sea stung his wounds. A battered crown, a few gems missing, left to him by a truly kind king—rarer than diamonds. A purple scarf, enchanted to never fade, still perfumed with spice, discovered in the highest tower of a ruined castle; perhaps it will outlast even him.

Four million seven hundred thousand three hundred and sixty-two. Done.

He surveys the oceans of rubies and mountains of gold, and despite their glow, restlessness prickles. As always, he had not left the cave while counting; as never before, it has taken so long that the outside world feels distant and bright. He turns away and lumbers toward the mouth of the tunnels, noticing as he scrapes along that either the mountain has shrunk or he has grown. Left, right, rise, dip—until a soft yellow light spills in, and fresh green scents ride the air.

He steps into a fine summer morning. The pines are deeper than any emerald, the rivers sparkling like flawless diamonds. He spreads his wings to warm them. Pebbles clatter. Wyrith cranes back toward the black doorway and, on a whim both practical and poetic, brings his tail down. Crack. Again. Crack. A final blow. The entrance collapses. His millennium-old hoard lies sealed behind boulders.

Well, he thinks, that will make a tale: The Noble Dragon’s Abandoned Riches. The Handsome Dragon’s Trove. Wyrith the Whimsical’s Hoard of Wonders. Yes—he likes that one.

He faces the edge, flares his wings, and considers possibilities: a kindly queen in need of a tower’s guardian; a century in the ocean’s gardens; a higher mountain; a company of adventurers and a string of quests; old friends. He tips forward and falls. The wind catches him. He is flying again, spinning and howling with laughter, centuries younger, light as a hatchling.

Wherever the wind takes him, he is ready to begin another adventure.
